request_debug.prompt_preview:
You rerank dead-startup candidates for a slopmortem pipeline. Given a fresh pitch and K candidates retrieved by hybrid search, score each candidate from four perspectives, then return ONLY the top 5 ranked from most to least relevant.

Perspectives (each scored 0-10 with a one-sentence rationale):
- business_model: how similar are the revenue, pricing, and customer-relationship shapes?
- market: how similar are the target buyers, market size, and demand drivers?
- gtm: how similar are the go-to-

response.text: {"ranked":[{"candidate_id":"ubiome::biotech","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":5,"rationale":"uBiome sold direct-to-consumer health testing kits with recurring elements, closer to a one-time purchase model than a monthly subscription, but still a B2C consumer health product sold directly to patients."},"market":{"score":7,"rationale":"Both target health-conscious consumers managing personal health conditions directly, with uBiome focusing on gut/microbiome health for individual consumers in a similar self-management niche."},"gtm":{"score":5,"rationale":"Both used direct-to-consumer digital channels targeting individual health consumers, though uBiome relied on kit sales and insurance billing rather than an app subscription model."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"uBiome raised $350K crowdfunding plus VC backing at early-to-mid stage before fraud collapsed it, somewhat comparable to an early-stage consumer healthtech startup."}},"rationale":"uBiome is the closest match as a direct-to-consumer health product targeting individual patients managing personal health metrics, though its revenue model (test kits + insurance billing) and fraud-driven failure differ from a subscription telehealth app."},{"candidate_id":"theranos::healthtech","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Theranos sold diagnostic tests through retail partnerships and insurance billing, not a B2C subscription model, making the revenue shape quite different from a monthly subscription bundle."},"market":{"score":6,"rationale":"Both targeted individual health consumers seeking accessible, convenient health monitoring outside traditional clinical settings, with a focus on democratizing healthcare access."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Theranos used retail pharmacy partnerships (Walgreens, Safeway) as its distribution channel, very different from a mobile app subscription GTM motion."},"stage_scale":{"score":3,"rationale":"Theranos reached a $9B valuation with hundreds of millions raised, far larger in scale than an early-stage consumer healthtech app at failure."}},"rationale":"Theranos shares the consumer healthtech sector and the vision of empowering patients with accessible health data, but the business model, GTM, and scale are substantially different from a subscription mobile app."},{"candidate_id":"jawbone::hardware","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Jawbone sold consumer hardware (fitness trackers) at one-time price points with no recurring subscription model, differing fundamentally from a monthly B2C subscription bundle."},"market":{"score":5,"rationale":"Jawbone's UP fitness trackers targeted health-conscious consumers monitoring personal health metrics, overlapping with the chronic illness self-management consumer audience."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"Jawbone sold through retail electronics channels and online stores, very different from a direct mobile app subscription go-to-market approach."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Jawbone raised over $900M and operated for years before liquidation in 2017, representing a much larger scale than an early-stage consumer healthtech app."}},"rationale":"Jawbone shares the consumer health wearable/tracking market focus but differs significantly in business model (hardware sales vs. subscription), GTM (retail vs. app store), and scale."},{"candidate_id":"pebble::hardware","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":2,"rationale":"Pebble was a hardware company selling smartwatches at one-time prices with a companion app ecosystem, not a recurring subscription healthtech service."},"market":{"score":4,"rationale":"Pebble targeted general tech-savvy consumers rather than chronic illness patients specifically, with only partial overlap in the health monitoring consumer segment."},"gtm":{"score":3,"rationale":"Pebble used crowdfunding and retail distribution channels, quite different from a direct mobile app subscription model targeting EU chronic illness patients."},"stage_scale":{"score":5,"rationale":"Pebble filed for insolvency in 2016 at a relatively modest scale ($23M acquisition price) after raising comparable amounts to an early-stage healthtech startup."}},"rationale":"Pebble has limited relevance to this pitch; it is a hardware company with a one-time purchase model targeting general consumers, sharing only a mobile companion app element and modest scale similarity."},{"candidate_id":"wonga::fintech","perspective_scores":{"business_model":{"score":3,"rationale":"Wonga operated a B2C lending platform with recurring customer relationships in a regulated EU market, offering some structural similarity as a consumer subscription-like recurring digital service in Europe."},"market":{"score":2,"rationale":"Wonga targeted financially stressed UK/EU consumers seeking short-term credit, a very different buyer profile from chronic illness patients seeking health management tools."},"gtm":{"score":4,"rationale":"Wonga used direct digital/online acquisition channels targeting individual consumers in the UK and Europe, somewhat analogous to a direct-to-consumer app subscription GTM in Germany and France."},"stage_scale":{"score":4,"rationale":"Wonga operated as a scaled European B2C digital consumer service before entering administration in 2018, offering some stage/scale comparability as a failed EU consumer digital product."}},"rationale":"Wonga is a weak match overall; it shares the EU B2C digital subscription-like recurring revenue and direct online GTM in Europe, but the market (fintech lending vs. chronic illness healthtech) and product differ fundamentally."}]}
